expanded_description: A clear introduction to viral replication cycles for secondary school biology. This covers how viruses take over host cells to make more viruses—from attaching to cells, entering, copying their genetic material, making new viral parts, assembling them, and escaping to infect new cells. It also explains the difference between viruses that kill cells quickly (lytic cycle) and those that hide inside the host (lysogenic cycle).

core_concept.principle: Viruses cannot reproduce on their own. They must infect a living cell and hijack its machinery to make new viruses. The replication cycle has seven steps: attachment, entry, uncoating, genome replication, gene expression, assembly, and release. Some viruses kill the cell immediately (lytic); others hide in the cell's DNA (lysogenic) and reactivate later.
core_concept.explanation:
Viruses are not alive. They cannot make energy, build proteins, or copy their genes on their own. To reproduce, they must get inside a living cell and use the cell's machinery. This is called the viral replication cycle.

The cycle starts when a virus attaches to a host cell. The virus has special proteins on its surface that fit into receptors on the cell like a key in a lock. This is why viruses only infect certain cells—HIV infects immune cells, influenza infects lung cells, and so on.

After attaching, the virus gets inside. Enveloped viruses (like influenza) fuse their membrane with the cell's membrane or are swallowed in a bubble (endocytosis). Bacteriophages (viruses that infect bacteria) inject their DNA like a tiny syringe. Non-enveloped viruses often get taken in by endocytosis.

Once inside, the virus must uncoat—remove its protein shell to release its genetic material. Then the virus takes over. It uses the cell's ribosomes to make viral proteins and its own enzymes (or the cell's) to copy its genome. DNA viruses often use the cell's DNA-copying machinery; RNA viruses usually bring their own copying enzymes.

The virus then assembles new viral particles. Capsid proteins self-assemble around the new genomes. Enveloped viruses wrap themselves in a piece of the cell's membrane as they bud out. Non-enveloped viruses often burst the cell to escape.

Some viruses have a different strategy. Instead of making new viruses right away, they insert their DNA into the host's DNA. This is called the lysogenic cycle. The viral DNA (now called a prophage) is copied every time the cell divides. It stays hidden until something triggers it to start the lytic cycle and make new viruses. Animal viruses can do something similar—herpesviruses hide in nerve cells for years, reactivating when the host is stressed.

Understanding viral replication helps us make antiviral drugs. We can block attachment (entry inhibitors), block copying of the genome (polymerase inhibitors), block protein cutting (protease inhibitors), or block release (neuraminidase inhibitors). It also helps us understand how viruses cause disease and how they spread.

image.alt: A diagram showing the seven steps of viral replication: attachment, entry, uncoating, genome replication, gene expression, assembly, release
image.caption: Viral replication cycle: 1. Attachment (virus binds cell), 2. Entry (virus gets inside), 3. Uncoating (capsid opens), 4. Genome replication (copying genes), 5. Gene expression (making proteins), 6. Assembly (building new viruses), 7. Release (viruses exit).

sections.introduction:
Viruses are the ultimate hijackers. They cannot reproduce on their own. They have no ribosomes to make proteins. They have no way to make energy. They are just a package of genetic material in a protein coat. To make more viruses, they must break into a living cell and take over.

This process is called the viral replication cycle. It happens in every infection, from the common cold to COVID-19 to HIV. Understanding this cycle helps us understand how viruses cause disease and how to stop them.

The cycle has seven steps. First, the virus attaches to the cell. It must find the right receptor—a protein on the cell surface that fits its spike like a key in a lock. This is why cold viruses infect nose cells, and flu viruses infect lung cells.

Second, the virus enters the cell. Some viruses fuse their envelope with the cell membrane. Others are swallowed in a bubble (endocytosis). Bacteriophages (viruses that infect bacteria) inject their DNA like a tiny syringe.

Third, the virus uncoats. It removes its protein coat to release its genetic material. Now the virus is ready to take over.

Fourth, the virus copies its genome. DNA viruses often use the cell's own DNA-copying machinery. RNA viruses usually bring their own copying enzymes. Some viruses (retroviruses like HIV) copy their RNA into DNA and then insert that DNA into the cell's chromosomes.

Fifth, the virus makes proteins. The cell's ribosomes read viral genes and make viral proteins. Some viruses shut down the cell's own protein production to focus on making viral proteins.

Sixth, the virus assembles new viral particles. Capsid proteins come together around the new genomes. Enveloped viruses wrap themselves in a piece of the cell's membrane.

Seventh, the virus is released. Some viruses burst the cell (lysis), killing it. Others bud out, taking a piece of membrane with them, and the cell may survive.

Some viruses have a different strategy. Instead of making new viruses right away, they hide. They insert their DNA into the cell's DNA and wait. This is called latency. Herpesviruses do this—they hide in nerve cells for years, then reactivate when the host is stressed.

Understanding these steps helps scientists design antiviral drugs. Each step is a potential target. Attachment inhibitors block the virus from binding. Polymerase inhibitors stop genome copying. Protease inhibitors prevent proteins from being cut into the right shapes. Release inhibitors stop viruses from escaping.

This section will explore each step of the replication cycle, how different viruses do it, and how we can stop them.

introduction_image.alt: A timeline of viral replication discoveries: one-step growth curve, lysogeny, reverse transcriptase, HIV integrase
introduction_image.caption: Viral replication discoveries: 1940s (phage one-step growth), 1950s (lysogeny), 1970s (reverse transcriptase), 1980s (HIV integration). Each discovery revealed new ways viruses replicate and new drug targets.

key_concept.definition: The viral replication cycle has seven steps: attachment, entry, uncoating, genome replication, gene expression, assembly, and release. Some viruses kill the host cell (lytic cycle); others insert their DNA into the host genome and hide (lysogenic cycle).
key_concept.context: Attachment is specific—viral spikes must bind host receptors. Entry can be by fusion (enveloped viruses), endocytosis, or injection (bacteriophages). Uncoating releases the genome. Genome replication uses host or viral polymerases. Gene expression produces viral proteins. Assembly packages genomes into capsids. Release occurs by lysis (cell bursts) or budding (cell survives). Lysogenic viruses integrate into host DNA, replicating passively until induced to enter the lytic cycle.

image.alt: Detailed diagram of the seven replication stages with molecular mechanisms shown
image.caption: Replication stages: 1. Attachment (spike binds receptor), 2. Entry (fusion/endocytosis/injection), 3. Uncoating (capsid opens), 4. Genome replication (copying), 5. Gene expression (making proteins), 6. Assembly (new viruses form), 7. Release (lysis or budding).

sections.key_concept: The viral replication cycle has seven steps. Some viruses follow the lytic cycle (make viruses and kill the cell). Others follow the lysogenic cycle (hide in the host DNA and wait). Animal viruses can also establish latency, hiding in cells for years.

classifications.value: Understanding attachment and entry explains why viruses only infect certain cells and how they get in.
characteristics: Attachment: Viral spikes bind to specific receptors on the host cell. The spike must fit the receptor like a key in a lock.
Receptor specificity determines host range (what species the virus infects) and tropism (what cells in the body).
Entry mechanisms: Fusion (enveloped viruses fuse with cell membrane), Endocytosis (cell swallows virus), Injection (bacteriophages inject DNA).
pH-dependent fusion: Some viruses (like influenza) need the acidic environment inside endosomes to trigger fusion.
Uncoating: The capsid opens, releasing the viral genome into the cell.
classifications.behavior:
The first step is attachment. The virus must find the right receptor. Influenza virus binds to sialic acid on lung cells. HIV binds to CD4 on immune cells. SARS-CoV-2 binds to ACE2 on many cell types. If the spike does not fit the receptor, the virus cannot infect.

After attachment, the virus gets inside. Enveloped viruses have two ways. Some (like HIV) fuse directly with the cell membrane. Others (like influenza) are swallowed into a bubble called an endosome. The acidic environment inside the endosome triggers the virus to fuse and release its contents.

Bacteriophages (viruses that infect bacteria) have a different strategy. They attach to the bacterial surface, then inject their DNA through the cell wall. The capsid stays outside. This is like a tiny syringe.

Once inside, the virus must uncoat. The capsid opens, releasing the genetic material. Some viruses uncoat at the cell membrane; others in the endosome; others in the nucleus. The genome is now free to start the infection.

examples.explanation:
Influenza virus has spikes called hemagglutinin (HA). HA binds to sialic acid on the surface of lung cells. The virus is then swallowed into an endosome.

Inside the endosome, the pH drops (becomes acidic). This low pH causes HA to change shape. A hidden part of HA (the fusion peptide) pops out and sticks to the endosome membrane. HA then folds back on itself, pulling the viral envelope and the endosome membrane together. They fuse. The viral RNA is released into the cell.

This pH-dependent mechanism ensures that the virus only fuses after it has been swallowed. It cannot fuse at the cell surface. This is a safety mechanism—if it fused too early, it might not get inside.

Influenza also has another spike called neuraminidase (NA). NA helps release new viruses by cutting sialic acid. This is the target of Tamiflu (oseltamivir).

image.alt: Influenza virus binding to sialic acid, endocytosis, low pH triggering fusion, and release of viral RNA
image.caption: Influenza entry: HA binds sialic acid → endocytosis → low pH triggers HA shape change → fusion peptide inserts → membranes fuse → viral RNA released. NA cuts sialic acid to help new viruses escape.

examples.explanation:
Bacteriophages are viruses that infect bacteria. They look like tiny spacecraft. They have a head (capsid) that holds DNA, a tail, and tail fibers.

When a phage finds a suitable bacterium, its tail fibers bind to receptors on the bacterial surface. The baseplate changes shape, and the tail sheath contracts. The tail tube is driven through the bacterial cell wall. The DNA is ejected from the head, through the tail tube, into the bacterium. The capsid stays outside.

This whole process takes less than a second. The phage does not need to be swallowed—it injects its DNA directly. This is efficient and fast.

Once the DNA is inside, it takes over the bacterium. The bacterium's machinery starts making new phages. Eventually, the bacterium bursts, releasing hundreds of new phages.

image.alt: Bacteriophage attaching to bacterium, tail sheath contracting, DNA being injected
image.caption: Phage injection: Tail fibers bind bacterium → sheath contracts → tail tube penetrates cell wall → DNA ejected into bacterium. Capsid stays outside. Entire process takes <1 second.

classifications.type: Genome Replication and Gene Expression: Taking Over
classifications.value: Understanding replication and expression explains how viruses make copies of themselves and take over the cell's machinery.
characteristics: DNA viruses: Often use the cell's DNA polymerase to copy their DNA. Replicate in the nucleus (except poxviruses).
RNA viruses: Use their own RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp) because cells do not have one. Replicate in the cytoplasm.
Retroviruses (HIV): Copy RNA into DNA using reverse transcriptase, then insert DNA into host genome using integrase.
Positive-sense RNA (+ssRNA): The genome itself is mRNA. Can be translated immediately. Examples: coronavirus, poliovirus.
Negative-sense RNA (-ssRNA): The genome must be copied into positive-sense RNA before translation. Virus must carry its own polymerase. Examples: influenza, Ebola.
Temporal regulation: Some viruses make early proteins (for replication) first, then late proteins (for assembly).
classifications.behavior:
Once the virus has uncoated, it must copy its genome and make proteins. How it does this depends on what kind of genome it has.

DNA viruses often use the cell's own DNA-copying machinery. They replicate in the nucleus, where the cell keeps its DNA. Herpesviruses do this. Poxviruses are an exception—they carry their own DNA polymerase and replicate in the cytoplasm.

RNA viruses have a problem. Cells do not have enzymes that copy RNA. So RNA viruses must bring their own—an RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp). They replicate in the cytoplasm, where they make copies of their RNA.

Positive-sense RNA viruses have an advantage. Their genome is already in the right form to be read as mRNA. As soon as it enters the cell, ribosomes can start making viral proteins. One of the first proteins made is the RdRp, which then copies the genome. Coronaviruses (including SARS-CoV-2) are +ssRNA viruses.

Negative-sense RNA viruses have a disadvantage. Their genome is the opposite of mRNA. It cannot be read directly. They must carry their own RdRp inside the virus particle. When the virus enters, the RdRp makes positive-sense copies first, then those are used to make proteins. Influenza is a -ssRNA virus.

Retroviruses (like HIV) are different. They have RNA, but they copy it into DNA using an enzyme called reverse transcriptase. The DNA then enters the nucleus and is inserted into the host genome by integrase. Once integrated, it is called a provirus. It stays there for life, and every time the cell divides, it copies the viral DNA. This is how HIV causes lifelong infection.

examples.explanation:
HIV is a retrovirus. It has an RNA genome. When it infects a cell, it must make DNA from that RNA. This is the opposite of what cells normally do (DNA → RNA). Hence the name reverse transcriptase.

Step 1: Reverse transcriptase copies the RNA into a single strand of DNA.

Step 2: It then copies that single strand into double-stranded DNA.

Step 3: The DNA enters the nucleus.

Step 4: Integrase cuts the host DNA and inserts the viral DNA. This is now called a provirus.

Once integrated, the viral DNA is part of the cell's genome. It is copied every time the cell divides. The cell may make new HIV viruses, or it may stay dormant. This is why HIV is so hard to cure—it hides in the DNA of infected cells.

Antiviral drugs target reverse transcriptase (AZT, tenofovir) and integrase (raltegravir). These drugs block the virus from making DNA or inserting it into the host genome.

image.alt: HIV reverse transcriptase making DNA from RNA, and integrase inserting viral DNA into host genome
image.caption: HIV replication: Reverse transcriptase copies RNA to DNA → DNA enters nucleus → integrase inserts viral DNA into host genome → provirus. Provirus hides in host DNA, making HIV hard to cure.

examples.process: Coronavirus Replication: Making a Giant RNA
examples.explanation:
SARS-CoV-2 (the virus that causes COVID-19) is a positive-sense RNA virus. Its genome is about 30,000 bases long—one of the largest RNA genomes.

When the virus enters a cell, its RNA is immediately read by ribosomes. They make two giant polyproteins. These are cut into smaller pieces by viral proteases. One of the pieces is the RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp).

The RdRp then copies the positive-sense RNA into negative-sense RNA. It uses the negative-sense RNA as a template to make many positive-sense copies. Some of these are full-length genomes for new viruses. Others are shorter messages that make the spike protein and other structural proteins.

The virus also makes a proofreading enzyme (nsp14). This fixes mistakes in the RNA, keeping the genome stable. This is unusual—most RNA viruses have high mutation rates. Coronavirus mutates more slowly because of this proofreading.

Antiviral drugs target the RdRp (remdesivir) and the protease (nirmatrelvir, part of Paxlovid). These drugs block replication and protein processing.

image.alt: Coronavirus genome translation, polyprotein processing, RdRp copying RNA, and subgenomic mRNA production
image.caption: Coronavirus replication: +ssRNA translated → polyproteins cut → RdRp copies RNA → makes negative-sense templates → produces new genomes and mRNAs. Proofreading enzyme (nsp14) fixes errors.

classifications.type: Assembly and Release: Making New Viruses
classifications.value: Understanding assembly and release explains how new viruses are built and how they escape to infect new cells.
characteristics: Assembly: Capsid proteins self-assemble around the viral genome. Enveloped viruses also incorporate spike proteins.
Maturation: Some viruses need to cut their proteins to become infectious. HIV protease cuts the Gag polyprotein into functional pieces.
Release by lysis: Non-enveloped viruses often burst the cell (lysis), releasing new viruses and killing the cell.
Release by budding: Enveloped viruses push through the cell membrane, wrapping themselves in a piece of it. The cell may survive.
Host factors: Viruses often hijack host machinery to help them bud. HIV uses the cell's ESCRT system to pinch off the membrane.
classifications.behavior:
After the viral genome has been copied and viral proteins have been made, new viruses must be assembled. Capsid proteins self-assemble into a shell around the genome. This happens spontaneously—the proteins are shaped to fit together.

Enveloped viruses also need to get their envelope. The envelope is a piece of the cell's membrane. The virus pushes against the membrane, wraps itself in it, and pinches off. This is called budding. The envelope is studded with viral spike proteins that were already inserted into the membrane.

Some viruses need to mature after they are released. HIV, for example, makes a long polyprotein that must be cut into pieces. The viral protease does this cutting. Without cutting, the virus is not infectious. This is why protease inhibitors (like those in HIV treatment) work—they block the cutting, and the virus cannot infect new cells.

Non-enveloped viruses do not bud. They often cause the cell to burst (lyse), releasing new viruses. This kills the cell. The burst cell releases hundreds or thousands of new viruses. This is why you feel sick during a viral infection—your cells are being killed.

examples.explanation:
HIV builds new viruses at the cell membrane. The viral Gag proteins assemble into a sphere just under the membrane. The viral RNA is packaged inside. The spike proteins (gp120/gp41) are already on the membrane.

The assembled virus pushes against the membrane. The cell's own machinery (called ESCRT) helps pinch off the membrane, releasing the virus. The virus is now wrapped in a piece of the cell's membrane.

But the new virus is not yet infectious. It has a long Gag-Pol polyprotein that must be cut into pieces. The viral protease does this cutting. It cuts the polyprotein into the functional proteins: capsid (CA), matrix (MA), nucleocapsid (NC), and the enzymes (protease, reverse transcriptase, integrase).

After cutting, the capsid rearranges into its final shape. The virus is now mature and can infect new cells. HIV drugs called protease inhibitors block this cutting, so the virus is released but cannot infect.

image.alt: HIV assembly at membrane, budding using ESCRT, and protease cutting to mature the virus
image.caption: HIV budding: Gag proteins assemble → push against membrane → ESCRT pinches off → virus released. Protease cuts Gag-Pol polyprotein → capsid matures → virus becomes infectious. Protease inhibitors block maturation.

examples.explanation:
Bacteriophages (phages) are viruses that infect bacteria. After they have made many new phages inside the bacterium, they need to get out. They do this by bursting the bacterium.

The phage makes a protein called holin. Holin makes holes in the inner membrane of the bacterium. Another protein, called endolysin, is waiting in the cytoplasm. When the holes open, endolysin rushes out and cuts the cell wall (peptidoglycan). The bacterium bursts (lyses), releasing hundreds of new phages.

The timing is precise. The holin opens at a specific time after infection—usually about 50 minutes. If it opened too early, the new phages would not be ready. If it opened too late, the bacterium might die from other causes. This timing is programmed into the phage's genes.

This lysis mechanism is used by many phages. It is also the basis for phage therapy—using phages to kill bacteria that are resistant to antibiotics.

image.alt: Bacteriophage holin forming pores, endolysin degrading cell wall, and bacterium bursting
image.caption: Phage lysis: Holin forms pores in inner membrane → endolysin escapes → degrades cell wall → bacterium bursts → phages released. Precise timing ensures new phages are ready before lysis.

classifications.value: Understanding the lysogenic cycle explains how some viruses can hide in cells for years, waiting to reactivate.
characteristics: Lysogenic cycle: Viral DNA is inserted into the host genome (prophage). It replicates passively when the cell divides.
Induction: Stress signals (UV light, chemicals) trigger the prophage to leave the genome and enter the lytic cycle.
Lysogenic conversion: Some prophages carry genes that benefit the host (e.g., toxin genes in bacteria).
Latency in animal viruses: Herpesviruses hide in nerve cells for years, reactivating when the host is stressed.
Provirus: HIV integrates into host DNA and can remain dormant (latent) for years.
classifications.behavior:
Some viruses do not immediately make new viruses. Instead, they insert their DNA into the host's DNA. This is called the lysogenic cycle. The viral DNA is now called a prophage (in bacteria) or a provirus (in animals).

The prophage is copied every time the cell divides. It stays hidden. It may stay there for generations. The virus is not making new viruses, and the host cell may not even know it is there.

Sometimes, something triggers the prophage to leave. UV light, chemicals, or stress can activate it. The prophage cuts itself out of the host DNA and starts the lytic cycle. It makes new viruses and kills the cell. This is called induction.

In bacteria, lysogenic viruses can carry genes that benefit the host. For example, the bacteria that cause diphtheria and cholera get their toxins from prophages. This is called lysogenic conversion. The virus and the bacteria have a kind of partnership—the virus gets a safe home, and the bacteria get new abilities.

In animals, many viruses can establish latency. Herpesviruses hide in nerve cells for years. When the host is stressed, they reactivate and cause cold sores or shingles. HIV integrates into the DNA of immune cells and can stay dormant for years. This is why these viruses are so hard to cure—the virus is hiding in the DNA, waiting.

examples.explanation:
Herpes simplex virus (HSV) causes cold sores. After the first infection, the virus does not go away. It hides.

During the initial infection, the virus infects skin cells and makes many copies. It also travels up nerve cells to the nerve ganglia near the spine. In these neurons, the virus stops making new viruses. It establishes latency. The viral DNA stays in the nucleus as a circular episome (not integrated). It makes a few RNA molecules called LATs (latency-associated transcripts) but no new viruses.

The virus can stay like this for years. When the host gets stressed (sunburn, fever, emotional stress), the virus reactivates. It starts making new viruses. These travel down the nerve to the skin and cause a cold sore. The sore sheds new viruses, which can infect other people.

This strategy allows the virus to survive for the lifetime of the host. It can reactivate many times, spreading to new people each time. Antiviral drugs (acyclovir) block the lytic cycle but do not eliminate the latent virus. This is why cold sores keep coming back.

image.alt: Herpes simplex infection: initial skin infection, transport to nerve ganglia, latency, stress-induced reactivation, and cold sore
image.caption: HSV latency: Initial infection → virus travels to nerve ganglia → latency (circular episome, LATs expressed) → stress triggers reactivation → virus travels to skin → cold sore. Latency allows lifelong infection.

examples.explanation:
Lambda is a bacteriophage that infects E. coli bacteria. It has a famous decision to make: lytic or lysogenic?

When lambda infects a bacterium, it can go either way. If the bacterium is healthy and growing well, lambda may choose lysogeny. It inserts its DNA into the bacterial chromosome and waits. The prophage is copied when the bacterium divides. The bacterium does not die.

If the bacterium is stressed (starving, damaged), lambda chooses the lytic cycle. It makes many copies of itself, then bursts the bacterium. This releases new phages to find healthier bacteria.

The decision is controlled by a protein called CII. If CII is high, the virus goes lysogenic. If CII is low, it goes lytic. CII levels depend on how healthy the bacterium is. In a healthy bacterium, CII is degraded slowly; in a stressed bacterium, it is degraded quickly.

This is a clever strategy. When times are good, the virus waits. When times are bad, it leaves. This maximizes its chances of survival.

image.alt: Lambda phage decision: high CII leads to lysogeny, low CII leads to lysis
image.caption: Lambda decision: Healthy bacteria → CII high → lysogeny (integrate, wait). Stressed bacteria → CII low → lysis (make phages, burst). Virus chooses based on host condition.

principles.principle: Receptor Specificity Determines Who Gets Infected
principles.statement: Viruses can only infect cells that have the right receptor. The viral spike must fit the host receptor like a key in a lock. This determines what species and what cells a virus can infect.
principles.application:
This is why HIV does not infect dogs, and why influenza viruses that infect birds do not usually infect humans. The spikes do not fit the receptors. If a mutation changes the spike so it fits a new receptor, the virus can jump to a new species. This is how pandemics start.

Receptor specificity also determines what cells in the body are infected. HIV infects immune cells because they have CD4. Influenza infects lung cells because they have sialic acid. SARS-CoV-2 infects many cells because ACE2 is on many tissues, which explains why COVID-19 can affect lungs, heart, and other organs.

This principle is why we can make vaccines that target the spike. If you block the spike with antibodies, the virus cannot attach. This is how most vaccines work.
principles.example: Influenza virus normally infects birds. Bird flu viruses have spikes that bind to sialic acid receptors found in bird intestines. To infect humans, the virus needs to change its spike so it binds to human sialic acid (found in the lungs). This happened in 1918, 1957, 1968, and 2009—each time, a new flu virus jumped from animals to humans, causing a pandemic. Scientists watch for these changes to predict the next pandemic.
principles.analogy: The viral spike is like a key. The receptor is like a lock. If the key fits, the door opens. If it does not fit, you cannot get in. Mutations can reshape the key so it fits a new lock (a new species). This is how viruses jump between species.

image.alt: Viral spikes binding to receptors: HIV to CD4, influenza to sialic acid, SARS-CoV-2 to ACE2
image.caption: Receptor specificity: HIV spike binds CD4 (immune cells), influenza spike binds sialic acid (lung cells), SARS-CoV-2 spike binds ACE2 (many cells). Key must fit lock to infect.

principles.principle: RNA Viruses Mutate Faster Than DNA Viruses
principles.statement: RNA viruses make mistakes when copying their RNA because their polymerases have no proofreading. DNA viruses have proofreading and make fewer mistakes. This affects how fast they evolve.
principles.application:
RNA viruses mutate quickly. This is why we need a new flu shot every year—the virus has changed enough that last year's vaccine may not work. HIV mutates so fast that it can become resistant to drugs within weeks. Coronaviruses have a proofreading enzyme, so they mutate more slowly than other RNA viruses, but still faster than DNA viruses.

DNA viruses mutate slowly. Smallpox, measles, and herpesviruses are DNA viruses. Once you get them or get vaccinated, you are usually protected for life. They do not change much over time.

This principle explains why some viruses are hard to vaccinate against (HIV, influenza) and why some are easy (measles, polio). It also explains why new variants of SARS-CoV-2 keep appearing—it is an RNA virus, so it mutates, but slower than flu because of its proofreading.
principles.example: Influenza virus mutates about 1-2% per year. This is enough to require a new vaccine each year. HIV mutates even faster—about 1% per replication cycle, which means within an infected person, the virus can evolve resistance to drugs in weeks. SARS-CoV-2 mutates about 0.1-0.2% per year, slower than flu but fast enough to generate new variants (Alpha, Delta, Omicron). Measles virus (DNA) mutates so slowly that the vaccine made in 1963 still works today.
principles.analogy: RNA viruses are like copying a book by hand with no spell-check. You will make many mistakes. DNA viruses are like using a photocopier with a built-in proofreader. The copies are much more accurate. More mistakes mean faster evolution, which means the virus can change to evade vaccines and drugs.

image.alt: Comparison of mutation rates: RNA viruses (high), DNA viruses (low), with examples
image.caption: Mutation rates: RNA viruses (flu, HIV, SARS-CoV-2) mutate quickly—need new vaccines often. DNA viruses (measles, herpes) mutate slowly—vaccines last for years. Proofreading enzymes affect rate.

principles.principle: Lytic vs. Lysogenic: A Strategic Choice
principles.statement: Some viruses have a choice: make new viruses immediately and kill the cell (lytic), or hide in the host DNA and wait (lysogenic). The choice depends on the health of the host and the environment.
principles.application:
When a virus infects a healthy, growing cell, it may choose to hide. The prophage will be copied every time the cell divides. The virus spreads without killing the host. When the host is stressed (UV light, starvation, immune attack), the virus may choose to activate. It makes new viruses, kills the cell, and looks for a new host.

This strategy helps the virus survive. If it killed every cell immediately, it might run out of hosts. By hiding, it can spread slowly and wait for the right moment to burst out.

Animal viruses use similar strategies. Herpesviruses hide in nerve cells for years, reactivating when the host is stressed. HIV hides in the DNA of immune cells, creating a latent reservoir that is very hard to eliminate.
principles.example: Chickenpox is a good example. The first infection causes chickenpox (lytic cycle). The virus then hides in nerve cells (latency). Years later, when the immune system weakens (stress, old age), the virus reactivates and causes shingles. This is the same virus, doing the same thing—making new viruses when conditions are right, hiding when they are not.
principles.analogy: Lytic is like a wildfire—burns fast, spreads, but then runs out of fuel. Lysogenic is like a slow burn—the virus hides in the wood, waiting for the right conditions to flare up. Both strategies have advantages. Viruses that do both are the most successful.

image.alt: Comparison of lytic cycle (immediate replication, cell death) and lysogenic cycle (integration, dormancy, later activation)
image.caption: Lytic vs. lysogenic: Lytic (immediate replication, cell bursts, rapid spread). Lysogenic (integrate into host DNA, hide, reactivate later). Viruses choose based on host condition.

components.definition: Viral replication requires viral spikes (for attachment), polymerases (for copying genomes), proteases (for processing proteins), and assembly machinery. The virus often hijacks host cell components to help.
components.interpretation: How fast a virus spreads depends on how well it attaches to cells, how quickly it copies its genome, and how effectively it releases new viruses. If any step is slow or inefficient, the virus will not spread well.

image.alt: Diagram of viral replication components: spikes, polymerase, protease, capsid proteins
image.caption: Replication components: Spikes (attachment), Polymerase (copying genome), Protease (cutting proteins), Capsid proteins (assembly). Each is a target for antiviral drugs.

categories.description: Antiviral drugs target specific steps in the replication cycle. Entry inhibitors block attachment; polymerase inhibitors block copying; protease inhibitors block processing; release inhibitors block exit.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Entry inhibitors: Maraviroc blocks the CCR5 receptor that HIV uses. Enfuvirtide blocks HIV fusion. These prevent the virus from getting into cells.

Polymerase inhibitors: Remdesivir mimics an RNA building block; when the virus's polymerase adds it to a new RNA chain, the chain stops. Acyclovir does the same for herpesvirus DNA. AZT does the same for HIV reverse transcriptase.

Protease inhibitors: HIV protease cuts the Gag-Pol polyprotein into functional pieces. Drugs like ritonavir block the protease, so the virus cannot mature. SARS-CoV-2 protease inhibitors (nirmatrelvir) work the same way.

Release inhibitors: Oseltamivir (Tamiflu) blocks influenza neuraminidase, which helps new viruses escape from cells. Without it, viruses get stuck on the cell surface.

Combination therapy (using several drugs at once) is used for HIV and hepatitis C to prevent resistance. If the virus mutates to escape one drug, the others still work.

examples.explanation:
Remdesivir is a drug used to treat COVID-19. It is a nucleoside analog—it looks like one of the building blocks of RNA (adenosine).

The virus's RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp) tries to add remdesivir to the growing RNA chain. But remdesivir has a chemical group that blocks further addition. After remdesivir is added, the polymerase can add only a few more bases, then it stops. The RNA chain is terminated.

This stops the virus from making new RNA copies. Without new genomes, the virus cannot spread. Remdesivir was originally developed for Ebola and hepatitis C, but it works against many RNA viruses because their polymerases are similar.

Resistance can occur if the virus mutates its polymerase so it no longer accepts remdesivir. This is why combination therapy is often used—using two drugs that target different steps makes it harder for the virus to escape.

image.alt: Remdesivir being incorporated into RNA chain, causing chain termination
image.caption: Remdesivir: Nucleoside analog that looks like adenosine. Viral polymerase adds it to RNA chain → chain stops growing → no new genomes. Targets RdRp of RNA viruses.

sections.section_title: Worked Example: Analyzing a Virus's Replication Strategy

worked_examples.title: Classifying a Virus from Its Replication Features
worked_examples.problem: You isolate a new virus. Tests show: (a) It has an RNA genome. (b) When you extract the RNA and put it into cells, it starts making viral proteins. (c) It has an envelope with spikes. (d) It replicates in the cytoplasm. (e) It buds from the cell membrane. (f) It makes a giant polyprotein that is cut by a viral protease. (1) What kind of RNA does it have? (2) How does it enter cells? (3) How does it copy its genome? (4) How does it get out? (5) What drugs might work against it?

step_by_step.explanation: Determine genome type from RNA infectivity.
step_by_step.calculation: Extracted RNA is infectious → it can act as mRNA. This means it is positive-sense RNA (+ssRNA). Negative-sense RNA would need to be transcribed first. This virus is a +ssRNA virus.
Enveloped with spikes → likely enters by membrane fusion. Could be at the cell surface or after endocytosis. The spikes will bind to a receptor on the host cell.

+ssRNA virus → genome is translated directly. It makes a giant polyprotein that is cut by a viral protease. One of the cut pieces is the RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp). The RdRp copies the genome, making new positive-sense RNA for new viruses and for making more proteins. This is typical of coronaviruses and other large +ssRNA viruses.
Enveloped virus that buds from the cell membrane → acquires envelope by budding. Spikes are inserted into the cell membrane, and the virus pushes through, wrapping itself in membrane.

Possible drug targets: (1) Entry inhibitors (block spike-receptor binding or fusion), (2) Protease inhibitors (block cutting of polyprotein), (3) RdRp inhibitors (remdesivir-like drugs). This virus is similar to coronaviruses, so drugs developed for COVID-19 might work.

worked_examples.final_answer: (1) Positive-sense RNA (+ssRNA). (2) Enters by membrane fusion (spike binds receptor, virus fuses with cell membrane). (3) Replicates by making a polyprotein, cutting it with protease, and using RdRp to copy RNA. (4) Buds from cell membrane, taking envelope with spikes. (5) Entry inhibitors, protease inhibitors, RdRp inhibitors. This shows how replication features reveal the virus's strategy and potential drug targets.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Viral replication strategy is determined by genome type, envelope status, and replication site. +ssRNA viruses can be translated immediately; they make polyproteins and use RdRp. Enveloped viruses enter by fusion and exit by budding. Each step can be targeted by antivirals.

sections.section_title: Process Flow: The Viral Replication Cycle
sections.introduction: The viral replication cycle proceeds step by step from attachment to release. Understanding this flow reveals how viruses hijack cells and how we can stop them.

process_flow.description: Viral spike binds to host cell receptor.
process_flow.mechanism: Spike proteins on virus surface recognize specific receptor proteins on host cell. Binding is specific—like key in lock. Determines host range and tissue tropism.
process_flow.result: Virus attached to cell; ready for entry.

image.alt: Viral spike binding to cell receptor
image.caption: Step 1: Attachment. Viral spike binds specific receptor on host cell. Key must fit lock. Determines what cells virus can infect.

process_flow.description: Virus enters cell by fusion, endocytosis, or injection.
process_flow.mechanism: Enveloped viruses: fuse with membrane (pH-dependent or independent). Non-enveloped: often taken in by endocytosis. Bacteriophages: inject DNA. Some viruses require low pH in endosome to trigger fusion.
process_flow.result: Viral genome inside cell; capsid may be intact or partially opened.

image.alt: Virus entering cell by fusion or endocytosis
image.caption: Step 2: Entry. Enveloped viruses fuse with membrane; non-enveloped often use endocytosis; phages inject DNA. Some require low pH to trigger fusion.

process_flow.mechanism: Capsid disassembles in response to cellular signals (low pH, proteases). Genome is released into cytoplasm or nucleus. Some viruses (herpes) deliver genome directly to nucleus.
process_flow.result: Viral genome free; ready for replication.

image.alt: Capsid opening, releasing viral RNA or DNA
image.caption: Step 3: Uncoating. Capsid opens in response to cellular signals (low pH, proteases). Genome released into cytoplasm or nucleus.

process_flow.description: Viral genome is copied; viral proteins are made.
process_flow.mechanism: DNA viruses use host polymerases in nucleus; RNA viruses use viral RdRp in cytoplasm; retroviruses reverse-transcribe RNA to DNA and integrate. Temporal regulation: early proteins first (replication enzymes), then late proteins (structural).
process_flow.result: Many copies of genome; viral proteins made.

image.alt: Viral genome being copied and viral proteins being made on ribosomes
image.caption: Step 4: Replication and expression. DNA viruses use host polymerases; RNA viruses use viral RdRp. Proteins made on host ribosomes. Early proteins (replication) first, then late proteins (structural).

process_flow.mechanism: Capsid proteins self-assemble around viral genomes. Enveloped viruses also incorporate spike proteins into membrane. Some viruses need maturation cleavage (protease) to become infectious.
process_flow.result: New viruses assembled; ready for release.

image.alt: Capsid proteins assembling around genomes, spike proteins in membrane
image.caption: Step 5: Assembly. Capsid proteins self-assemble around genomes. Enveloped viruses incorporate spike proteins from membrane. Some viruses need protease cutting to mature.

process_flow.mechanism: Enveloped viruses bud from membrane, taking a piece with them. Non-enveloped viruses often cause cell lysis (bursting). Some viruses use host machinery (ESCRT) to help budding.
process_flow.result: New viruses released; can infect new cells.

image.alt: Enveloped virus budding from membrane; non-enveloped virus causing cell lysis
image.caption: Step 6: Release. Enveloped viruses bud (take piece of membrane); non-enveloped viruses often burst cell (lysis). Release inhibitors block escape (oseltamivir).

sections.section_title: Advanced Concepts: Viral Replication and Medicine
sections.definition: Understanding viral replication has led to vaccines, antiviral drugs, and even the use of viruses to treat bacterial infections (phage therapy).

process_types.energy_profile: Vaccines use harmless parts of viruses to teach the immune system to recognize and block the real virus.
process_types.direction: Vaccine (spike protein or weakened virus) → immune system makes antibodies and memory cells → protection against real virus
process_types.function: Prevent viral infections by preparing the immune system before exposure
goals: Induce neutralizing antibodies against viral spikes
process_types.energy_source: Immune system uses energy to make antibodies and memory cells; vaccines provide the blueprint.

primary_example.explanation:
The COVID-19 mRNA vaccines (Pfizer, Moderna) use the virus's spike protein to train the immune system. The vaccine contains mRNA that codes for the spike protein. When injected, the mRNA enters cells, and the cells make the spike protein. The immune system sees the spike and makes antibodies and memory cells. If the person later encounters the real virus, the immune system is ready.

The spike protein in the vaccine is modified to stay in its prefusion shape. This shape is what antibodies best recognize. The structure of the spike was determined by cryo-electron microscopy, guiding the design.

These vaccines do not contain live virus, so they cannot cause COVID-19. They have been highly effective at preventing severe disease.

image.alt: mRNA vaccine entering cell, making spike protein, immune response
image.caption: mRNA vaccine: mRNA enters cell → cell makes spike protein → immune system makes antibodies → protection. Uses viral replication steps (gene expression) without infection.

image.alt: Different vaccine types: mRNA, viral vector, subunit, inactivated virus
image.caption: Vaccine types: mRNA (spike protein made in cell), Viral vector (harmless virus delivers spike gene), Subunit (spike protein alone), Inactivated (whole virus killed). All train immune system to recognize virus.

process_types.type: Phage Therapy: Using Viruses to Kill Bacteria
process_types.energy_profile: Bacteriophages (viruses that infect bacteria) can be used to treat bacterial infections, especially those resistant to antibiotics.
process_types.direction: Phage infects bacteria → replicates → lyses bacteria → more phages → kills bacterial population
process_types.function: Treat antibiotic-resistant bacterial infections using viruses that specifically target bacteria
goals: Target specific bacteria (phages are very specific)
Kill bacteria that are resistant to antibiotics
process_types.energy_source: Phages replicate using bacterial machinery; no energy input needed.

primary_example.explanation:
Some bacteria have become resistant to all antibiotics. Phage therapy uses viruses that infect those bacteria to treat infections. The phages are specific—they only infect the target bacteria, not human cells or beneficial bacteria.

The phages are applied to the infection site. They attach to the bacteria, inject their DNA, and replicate. The bacteria are killed when the phages burst out (lysis). The new phages go on to infect more bacteria. The process continues until the bacterial population is eliminated.

Phage therapy has been used for decades in Eastern Europe and is now being developed in the West. It has been used to treat infections that did not respond to any antibiotics, saving patients' lives.

Phages can also break down biofilms—protective layers that bacteria form on medical devices. This makes them useful for treating chronic infections.

image.alt: Phage infecting bacterium, replicating, lysing bacterium, and new phages spreading
image.caption: Phage therapy: Phage infects bacteria → replicates → bursts bacteria → releases more phages → kills bacterial population. Used for antibiotic-resistant infections.

image.alt: Phage therapy applications: wound infections, lung infections, biofilm removal
image.caption: Phage therapy uses: wound infections, lung infections (cystic fibrosis), biofilm removal from medical devices. Specific to target bacteria, does not harm human cells.

sections.relationship: Vaccines and phage therapy both use our understanding of viral replication. Vaccines teach the immune system to recognize viral spikes. Phage therapy uses viruses' natural ability to kill bacteria. Both are tools to fight infections.

sections.purpose: These are the essential vocabulary terms for understanding viral replication. Knowing these terms will help you describe how viruses infect cells and make more viruses.

activation.definition: The terms below cover the most important concepts in viral replication. Each term is defined simply, with context to help you remember its significance.
activation.effect: Mastering these terms will allow you to discuss how viruses reproduce, how they cause disease, and how we can stop them.

image.alt: A diagram of the viral replication cycle with key terms: attachment, entry, uncoating, replication, assembly, release
image.caption: Key replication terms: Knowing these steps helps you understand how viruses hijack cells and how to stop them.

key_properties.explanation: The virus binds to a receptor on the host cell. Viral spikes must fit the receptor like a key in a lock. Determines what cells the virus can infect.
The virus gets inside the cell. Enveloped viruses fuse with the cell membrane or are taken in by endocytosis. Bacteriophages inject their DNA. Non-enveloped viruses often use endocytosis.
The capsid opens, releasing the viral genome. May happen at the cell membrane, in an endosome, or in the nucleus.
The viral genome is copied. DNA viruses often use host polymerases; RNA viruses use their own RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp). Retroviruses reverse-transcribe RNA to DNA.
Viral proteins are made on host ribosomes. Positive-sense RNA viruses can be translated immediately; negative-sense RNA viruses must be transcribed first.
New viral particles are built. Capsid proteins self-assemble around the genome. Enveloped viruses incorporate spike proteins from the host membrane.
New viruses exit the cell. Enveloped viruses bud (take a piece of membrane). Non-enveloped viruses often cause cell lysis (bursting).
Virus replicates immediately and kills the host cell. Produces many new viruses quickly.
Viral DNA is inserted into the host genome (prophage). It replicates when the cell divides. Later, it may be induced to enter the lytic cycle.
In animal viruses, the virus hides in cells without making new viruses. Herpesviruses hide in nerve cells; HIV hides in immune cells. Can reactivate later.
A protein on the host cell surface that the virus binds to. Determines host range and tissue tropism. Examples: CD4 (HIV), ACE2 (SARS-CoV-2), sialic acid (influenza).
RNA that can be directly read by ribosomes as mRNA. The genome itself is infectious. Examples: coronavirus, poliovirus, hepatitis C.
RNA that must be copied into positive-sense RNA before translation. The virus must carry its own RNA-dependent RNA polymerase. Examples: influenza, Ebola, rabies.
An enzyme that copies RNA into DNA. Used by retroviruses (HIV). Target of AZT and other HIV drugs.
An enzyme that inserts viral DNA into the host genome. Used by retroviruses (HIV). Target of raltegravir and other HIV drugs.
An enzyme that cuts viral polyproteins into functional pieces. Target of HIV protease inhibitors (ritonavir) and SARS-CoV-2 protease inhibitors (nirmatrelvir).
An enzyme that copies RNA from an RNA template. Used by RNA viruses. Target of remdesivir and other antiviral drugs.
The process by which enveloped viruses acquire their envelope. The virus pushes through the cell membrane, wrapping itself in a piece of it.
The bursting of a cell to release new viruses. Used by bacteriophages and many non-enveloped animal viruses.
Viral DNA that has been inserted into the host genome. Replicated when the cell divides. HIV provirus hides in immune cells.

summary:
Viruses cannot reproduce on their own. They must infect a living cell and hijack its machinery to make new viruses. The replication cycle has seven steps: attachment, entry, uncoating, genome replication, gene expression, assembly, and release.

Attachment is specific—viral spikes must bind to host receptors like a key in a lock. This determines what species and what cells the virus can infect. Entry can be by fusion (enveloped viruses), endocytosis, or injection (bacteriophages). Uncoating releases the viral genome.

Genome replication depends on the virus type. DNA viruses often use the cell's DNA polymerases. RNA viruses use their own RNA-dependent RNA polymerases (RdRp) because cells do not have them. Retroviruses (HIV) copy RNA to DNA using reverse transcriptase, then insert the DNA into the host genome using integrase.

Gene expression makes viral proteins. Positive-sense RNA viruses can be translated immediately; negative-sense RNA viruses must be transcribed first. Temporal regulation ensures that replication enzymes are made before structural proteins.

Assembly builds new viruses. Capsid proteins self-assemble around the genome. Enveloped viruses bud through the cell membrane, taking a piece of it as their envelope. Release occurs by lysis (cell bursts) or budding (cell survives).

Some viruses can hide. The lysogenic cycle (in bacteria) inserts viral DNA into the host genome (prophage), where it replicates passively. Induction triggers the prophage to enter the lytic cycle. Animal viruses can establish latency—herpesviruses hide in nerve cells; HIV hides in immune cells. These viruses can reactivate years later.

Understanding viral replication has led to vaccines and antiviral drugs. mRNA vaccines teach the immune system to recognize viral spikes. Antiviral drugs target specific steps: entry inhibitors (block attachment/fusion), polymerase inhibitors (block genome copying), protease inhibitors (block protein processing), and release inhibitors (block escape). Phage therapy uses bacteriophages to kill antibiotic-resistant bacteria.

Understanding viral replication helps us understand how viruses cause disease, how they spread, and how to stop them.
